perturbation.original_text: 6.4 The Professional agrees to maintain at all times during the Term such insurance, including without limitation, health insurance, workers' compensation, automobile and general comprehensive liability coverage, as will protect and hold harmless the Company from any claims, losses, damages, costs, expenses or liability arising out of the Services performed under this Agreement. The Company may require the Professional to provide insurance certificates evidencing the same.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.4 The Professional agrees to maintain insurance as they deem necessary. The Company may request the Professional to provide information regarding any active insurance policies.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific types of insurance required (health, workers' compensation, automobile, and comprehensive liability), the modified text contradicts the requirement for independent contractors to maintain adequate insurance coverage to protect the hiring company from liability, which is often legally mandated. Furthermore, the change from 'insurance certificates' to 'information regarding any active insurance policies' reduces the burden of proof and enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 29 CFR § 1904.39 - Recording criteria for work-related injuries and illnesses.
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission of mandatory workers' compensation insurance directly contradicts OSHA regulations which require employers (or entities engaging contractors) to record and report work-related injuries and illnesses. Without proper insurance, the company may be liable for costs associated with the professional's injuries obtained in connection with the agreement, and the absence of a clear workers' compensation policy violates the spirit and intent of the recording and reporting requirements under 29 CFR § 1904.39.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1904.39 - Reporting fatalities, hospitalizations, amputations, and losses of an eye as a result of work-related incidents to OSHA. | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content Home By Standard Number 1904.39 - Reporting fatalities, hospitalizations, amputations, and losses of an eye as a result of work-related incidents to OSHA. Part Number: 1904 Part Number Title: Recording and Reporting Occupational Injuries and Illnesses Subpart: 1904 Subpart E Subpart Title: Reporting Fatality, Injury and Illness Information to the Government Standard Number: 1904.39 Title: Reporting fatalities, hospitalizations, amputations, and losses of an eye as a result of work-related incidents to OSHA. GPO Source: e-CFR 1904.39(a) Basic requirement. 1904.39(a)(1) Within eight (8) hours after the death of any employee as a result of a work-related incident, you must report the fatality to the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), U.S. Department of Labor. 1904.39(a)(2) Within twenty-four (24) hours after the in-patient hospitalization of one or more employees or an employee's amputation or an employee's loss of an eye, as a result of a work-related incident, you must report the in-patient hospitalization, amputation, or loss of an eye to OSHA. 1904.39(a)(3) You must report the fatality, inpatient hospitalization, amputation, or loss of an eye using one of the following methods: 1904.39(a)(3)(i) By telephone or in person to the OSHA Area Office that is nearest to the site of the incident. 1904.39(a)(3)(ii) By telephone to the OSHA toll-free central telephone number, 1-800-321-OSHA (1-800-321-6742). 1904.39(a)(3)(iii) By electronic submission using the reporting application located on OSHA's public Web site at www.osha.gov . 1904.39(b) Implementation - 1904.39(b)(1) If the Area Office is closed, may I report the fatality, in-patient hospitalization, amputation, or loss of an eye by leaving a message on OSHA's answering machine, faxing the Area Office, or send
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific insurance types (health, workers' compensation, etc.) to protect the company from liabilities. The changed text omits these specific requirements, stating only that the Professional agrees to maintain insurance as they deem necessary. The Law Explanation states that omitting workers' compensation contradicts OSHA regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 (1904.39) outlines the requirements for reporting work-related incidents, including fatalities and hospitalizations, to OSHA. By removing the requirement for worker's compensation, the changed text shifts the responsibility for covering work-related injuries onto the company, potentially violating OSHA regulations and creating a clear legal contradiction as the original contract provided protection from such liabilities, which is now removed.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 If the Company elects to create and market the Products, the Company will pay to the Professional a "Royalty Fee" on the sales of Products during the Term, except as provided in the following sentence, of two percent (2%) of the "Royalty Base," which Royalty Base shall be calculated as the wholesale selling price of all Products for which the Company actually receives the proceeds of such net of returns, allowances, discounts, shipping, taxes, insurance and credits. During the Term, the Company shall pay the Royalty Fee, earned for the preceding quarter, to the Professional quarterly, within thirty (30) days of the end of the succeeding calendar year quarter. If the Company decides not to renew this Agreement in accordance with the provisions of Section 8 below, the Company shall pay the Professional an amount equal to two percent (2%) of the net book value of its unsold inventory of Products on December 31, 2004.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 If the Company elects to create and market the Products, the Company will pay to the Professional a Royalty Fee on the sales of Products during the Term, as determined at the company's discretion. The Company shall pay the Royalty Fee at a time determined by the company. If the Company decides not to renew this Agreement in accordance with the provisions of Section 8 below, the Company may pay the Professional an amount of its choosing for its unsold inventory of Products on December 31, 2004.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the fixed royalty percentage (2%) and the specific calculation method, giving the company complete discretion over the royalty amount and payment schedule. This omission introduces legal risk as it removes a clear and agreed-upon compensation structure, potentially violating state wage and contract laws regarding fair compensation for services rendered, particularly if the professional is classified as an employee.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text specifies a clear royalty calculation and payment schedule, while the modified text gives the Company complete discretion. N.J. Stat. § 34:11-4.2 requires that wages be paid in a consistent manner, and gives the employee the right to be clearly informed of the terms of their employment.  The vagueness of the modified text could be interpreted as a violation of this statute, particularly if the professional is misclassified as an independent contractor but functions more like an employee.
perturbation.justification: The modified text gives the company complete discretion over royalty payments, which contradicts the principle that employees must be clearly informed of their employment terms and paid in a consistent manner. N.J. Stat. § 34:11-4.2 (as described in the Law Explanation) mandates clear employment terms, which the changed text fails to meet. By removing the fixed royalty percentage and payment schedule, the modified contract introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance with state wage laws regarding fair compensation. The omission of essential compensation details creates a legally problematic situation.

perturbation.original_text: 11.4. GOVERNING LAW. The validity, interpretation, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed in accordance with the laws of the State of New Jersey without giving effect to the principles of conflicts of laws of such state.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.4. GOVERNING LAW. The validity, interpretation, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed in accordance with principles determined by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies New Jersey law as the governing law. The modified text removes this and states that the agreement will be governed by principles determined by the company. This is problematic because it allows the company to potentially avoid consumer protection laws or other mandatory legal provisions that would apply under New Jersey law, which creates a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: N.J. Stat. § 56:12-15 explicitly prohibits consumers from waiving their rights under the New Jersey Consumer Fraud Act. By removing the governing law and allowing the company to define the governing principles, the contract effectively attempts to allow the company to waive application of the consumer protection that would be afforded under New Jersey state law. This is a contradiction.

Previous Next 56:12-15. Consumer contract, warranty, notice or sign; violation of legal right of consumer or responsibility of seller, lessor, etc.; prohibition; exemptions No seller, lessor, creditor, lender or bailee shall in the course of his business offer to any consumer or prospective consumer or enter into any written consumer contract or give or display any written consumer warranty, notice or sign after the effective date of this act which includes any provision that violates any clearly established legal right of a consumer or responsibility of a seller, lessor, creditor, lender or bailee as established by State or Federal law at the time the offer is made or the consumer contract is signed or the warranty, notice or sign is given or displayed. Consumer means any individual who buys, leases, borrows, or bails any money, property or service which is primarily for personal, family or household purposes. The provisions of this act shall not apply to residential leases or to the sale of real estate, whether improved or not, or to the construction of new homes subject to "The New Home Warrant
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts N.J. Stat. § 56:12-15. The original contract specifies New Jersey law as the governing law. The modified text removes this and states that the agreement will be governed by principles determined by the company. This allows the company to potentially avoid consumer protection laws that would apply under New Jersey law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states: 'No seller... shall... enter into any written consumer contract... which includes any provision that violates any clearly established legal right of a consumer... as established by State or Federal law...'. By removing the governing law and allowing the company to define the governing principles, the contract effectively attempts to allow the company to waive application of the consumer protection that would be afforded under New Jersey state law, which is a violation.
